planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs or rooted cuttings in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Carolina allspice blooms most prolifically in full sun but tolerates significant shade and is one of the few large flowering shrubs that performs well in light to moderate shade.

directions: Plant container-grown shrubs or rooted cuttings in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Carolina allspice blooms most prolifically in full sun but tolerates significant shade and is one of the few large flowering shrubs that performs well in light to moderate shade. Calycanthus floridus is a deciduous native shrub of the southeastern United States with a rounded, spreading habit and remarkable, deeply fragrant, strap-petaled flowers with brick-red to burgundy petals that resemble no other flower in the garden; blooming from late spring through summer, the flowers have a complex, spicy-sweet scent compared variously to strawberries, banana, and pineapple, and the aromatic quality extends to the crushed leaves and bark. The plant suckers gently from the base over time and can be used as an informal hedge, woodland shrub border specimen, or under-planted beneath large trees; it is completely carefree once established in zones 4–9.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs or rooted cuttings in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Florida anise is one of the finest native evergreen shrubs for deep shade in the Gulf South and grows naturally along shaded stream banks in Florida and adjacent states.

directions: Plant container-grown shrubs or rooted cuttings in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Florida anise is one of the finest native evergreen shrubs for deep shade in the Gulf South and grows naturally along shaded stream banks in Florida and adjacent states. Illicium floridanum is a large, evergreen native shrub or small tree with glossy, dark-green, elliptic leaves 3–6 inches long that are remarkably aromatic when bruised, smelling strongly of anise or licorice; in mid- to late spring it produces unusual, star-shaped, maroon-red to burgundy flowers with numerous strap-like petals that are fascinating if not conventionally beautiful, and the flowers are followed by star-shaped seed capsules similar to (but not the same as) culinary star anise. It forms a naturally dense, multi-stemmed shrub that is excellent for hedges, screens, and foundation plantings in shaded sites in zones 7–10; all parts of the plant are toxic.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in moist to wet, humus-rich, acidic soil in full to partial shade; small anise tree is more shade-tolerant and faster-growing than Florida anise (I. floridanum) and is especially effective as a tall, screening shrub in deep shade situations.

directions: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in moist to wet, humus-rich, acidic soil in full to partial shade; small anise tree is more shade-tolerant and faster-growing than Florida anise (I. floridanum) and is especially effective as a tall, screening shrub in deep shade situations. Illicium parviflorum is a large, fast-growing, evergreen native shrub or small tree endemic to central Florida and historically occurring only in Volusia and Seminole counties, though now widely cultivated throughout the Gulf South for its outstanding performance as a screening plant in difficult shaded and wet conditions; it produces bright, lime-green to medium-green, aromatic, elliptic leaves and tiny, inconspicuous, pale yellowish flowers in spring. A workhorse screening shrub and hedge plant for zones 7–10 that can reach 8–15 feet tall quickly; it naturalizes along shaded stream banks, pond margins, and moist ravines.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in moist, well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; needle palm is remarkably slow-growing — 5–10 years to a mature specimen — but extremely cold-hardy for a palm, often surviving temperatures down to 5°F when established.

directions: Plant container-grown plants in spring in moist, well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; needle palm is remarkably slow-growing — 5–10 years to a mature specimen — but extremely cold-hardy for a palm, often surviving temperatures down to 5°F when established. Rhapidophyllum hystrix is the hardiest palm in the world, a southeastern US native found naturally in wet flatwoods and hammocks from South Carolina to Florida and Mississippi; it grows as a low, clustering, trunkless to very short-trunked palm with fan-shaped, dark-green fronds on stout stems armed with long, sharp, black needles (giving rise to the common name) that create a nearly impenetrable clump reaching 3–6 feet tall and wide over many years. Despite its slow growth, it is a spectacular specimen palm for zones 7–10 shaded garden borders, and its extreme cold hardiness makes it the top choice for gardeners in zone 7 who want a true, large fan palm; once established it requires virtually no care.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; Mediterranean fan palm is drought-tolerant and salt-tolerant once established, making it an excellent landscape palm for coastal gardens in zones 8–11.

directions: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; Mediterranean fan palm is drought-tolerant and salt-tolerant once established, making it an excellent landscape palm for coastal gardens in zones 8–11. Chamaerops humilis is the only palm native to continental Europe, occurring naturally along the Mediterranean coast from the Iberian Peninsula to Italy and North Africa; it is a slow-growing, multi-stemmed clustering palm with silvery-green to blue-green, stiff fan-shaped fronds on petioles armed with sharp spines, forming a bold, architectural clump 4–10 feet tall over many years. It is highly versatile in the Gulf Coast landscape, functioning as a specimen plant, foundation plant, or tall screening shrub; it tolerates drought, salt spray, and brief cold spells to about 15°F when established.

planting.note: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; the Neches River rose mallow is a federally threatened species endemic to the Neches River bottomlands of East Texas and should only be purchased from reputable native plant nurseries — do not collect from the wild.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; the Neches River rose mallow is a federally threatened species endemic to the Neches River bottomlands of East Texas and should only be purchased from reputable native plant nurseries — do not collect from the wild. Hibiscus dasycalyx is one of the rarest members of the North American rose mallow complex; it closely resembles scarlet rose mallow (H. coccineus) and swamp rose mallow (H. moscheutos) in growth habit — producing tall, cane-like stems with deeply palmately lobed leaves — but bears distinctive white flowers with a dark wine-red center eye and a deeply pubescent calyx covered in long, soft hairs that distinguish it from its relatives. Although rare in nature, it is adaptable in cultivation to any wet, sunny site in zones 7–10 and performs well in rain gardens, pond margins, and moist meadows; it is a stunning, large-flowered native rose mallow of significant conservation interest.
